region_of_origin: Singapore (Vietnamese-origin dish adapted for Singapore market)

ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn entry exists specifically for squid and pork mince noodle soup (hu tieu). FOODON_00002473 (noodle soup food product) is the closest defensible parent class.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0FHR (Noodle soup) is the closest parent. No specific code exists for Vietnamese-style hu tieu with squid and pork mince in the FoodEx2 catalogue.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No suitable SNOMED CT concept found for this specific noodle soup dish.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard bowl of squid and pork mince noodle soup, approximately 450 g, as typically served at a Singapore Vietnamese restaurant.

nutrient_source.title: Estimated from component analysis: rice noodle soup with seafood and minced pork, adapted from USDA FoodData Central entries for rice noodles (SR Legacy 20444), cooked squid (SR Legacy 15149), and cooked ground pork (SR Legacy 10219), combined with broth and vegetable components
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service — FoodData Central (SR Legacy)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No exact entry for hu tieu muc thit bam exists in HPB Singapore Foodo, ICMR-NIN, or FAO/INFOODS SEA databases. Nutrient values are estimated from weighted component analysis using USDA SR Legacy data for rice noodles (~40% by weight), squid (~15%), minced pork (~15%), broth (~25%), and vegetables/condiments (~5%). Per-100g values reflect the composite dish as served in a bowl. Energy calculated as 4×protein + 4×carbohydrate + 9×fat.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — rice noodles as analogous base carbohydrate
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / American Journal of Clinical Nutrition
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for hu tieu muc thit bam. GI of 53 is estimated using rice noodles as the primary glycemic carbohydrate source (GI ~53, low category, per University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. 2021 international GI tables). Glycemic load per serving calculated as (GI × available carbohydrate per serving) / 100 = (53 × 44.1 g carbohydrate in 450 g serving) / 100 ≈ 23.4, rounded to 23.0. The protein and fat content of the dish further attenuates the postprandial glycemic response.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content from broth and fish sauce
hypertension.guidance: A single 450 g bowl provides approximately 1890 mg sodium, representing 82–94% of the WHO recommended daily limit of 2000 mg; individuals with hypertension should request reduced-sodium broth, limit the soup liquid, and avoid adding extra fish sauce or soy sauce.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Moderate glycemic load from rice noodles, partially offset by protein and fat
type2_diabetes.guidance: The estimated glycemic load of approximately 23 per serving is moderate; individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should consider a smaller portion of noodles, request extra vegetables and protein, and monitor postprandial blood glucose as rice noodles have a GI of approximately 53.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Elevated sodium and moderate saturated fat from pork and broth
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: The high sodium content (~1890 mg per bowl) and moderate saturated fat (~3.2 g per bowl) are relevant cardiovascular risk factors; individuals with cardiovascular disease should limit portion size, avoid consuming the full broth, and choose this dish only occasionally within a heart-healthy dietary pattern.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Squid and pork mince noodle soup, known in Vietnamese as Hủ tiếu mực thịt bằm (or colloquially Hủ tíu mực thịt bằm), is a Vietnamese-origin noodle soup dish widely served in Singapore at Vietnamese restaurants. The dish features thin, translucent rice noodles (hu tieu noodles) served in a clear, savoury pork bone broth that is seasoned with fish sauce, garlic, and shallots. The toppings include tender sliced squid (sotong in Malay/Singaporean English), seasoned minced pork, bean sprouts, spring onions, and fresh chilli. The broth is typically light and clean in flavour with a subtle sweetness from the pork bones, contrasting with the umami richness of the squid and the savoury depth of the minced pork. The noodles have a soft, slightly chewy texture. The dish is a popular lunch and dinner option in Singapore's Vietnamese dining establishments and is closely related to the Southern Vietnamese street food classic hu tieu Nam Vang (Phnom Penh-style noodle soup). Nutritionally, the dish is moderate in energy (~78 kcal per 100 g, ~351 kcal per bowl), provides meaningful protein from squid and pork (~6.2 g per 100 g), and has a moderate glycemic load due to the rice noodle base. The primary clinical concern is sodium, as the broth contributes substantially to daily sodium intake. The dish is not suitable for halal or kosher diets due to the pork content.

confidence_reason: No exact entry for this dish exists in HPB Singapore Foodo, ICMR-NIN, FAO/INFOODS SEA, or USDA databases. Nutrient values are estimated from weighted component analysis using USDA SR Legacy data for the primary ingredients (rice noodles, squid, minced pork, broth, vegetables). GI is estimated from rice noodles as the primary glycemic carbohydrate source per the University of Sydney GI Database. Confidence is medium rather than low because the component-level data sources are high quality and the estimation methodology is transparent and defensible.
notes: Nutrient values are component-estimated from USDA SR Legacy data; no direct analytical measurement of this composite dish was available in any approved primary source. Vitamin A, B-vitamins (B1, B2, B3, B6, B12), folate, vitamin D, vitamin E, phosphorus, magnesium, zinc, and selenium are reported as -1 because these micronutrients are not reported in the component-level USDA SR Legacy entries used for estimation and cannot be reliably summed without introducing unacceptable uncertainty. Trans fat is reported as 0.0 (confirmed zero) as the dish contains no hydrogenated fats. The dish contains pork and is therefore not halal or kosher. The Vietnamese name Hủ tíu reflects the Southern Vietnamese/Mekong Delta pronunciation variant of Hủ tiếu. The dish is served at Thien Long Vietnam Restaurant on Circular Road, Singapore (GrabFood source). Price SGD 13.90 as of data collection. Sodium estimate of 420 mg per 100 g is based on typical Vietnamese pork broth sodium levels and fish sauce seasoning; actual sodium may vary significantly by restaurant preparation.
